title: SplitTopologicalOrder
tex_block_html: For a CADMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$, also $G_{\spl(W)}$ is acyclic. If $<$ is any topological order of $G$ given by enumerating all nodes $v \in J \cup V$ via: \[ v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_n,\] then, for instance, a topological order for $G_{\spl(W)}$ can be achieved by assigning for a node $v_j \in W$ with index $j$ the node $v_j^0$ the index $j-\frac{1}{3}$ and $v_j^1$ the index $j+\frac{1}{3}$, and then ordering all nodes according to their index value.

% title: SplitTopologicalOrder
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_6}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_6}

\begin{Rem}[SplitTopologicalOrder]
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CADMG, i.e.\ a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}) that is acyclic in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic} (equivalently, $G$ satisfies the attribute ``CADMG'' of def \ref{def-cdmg-types}, item~i.); throughout we use the notation of def \ref{not-cdmg}, the family-relationship notation of def \ref{def_3_5}, the topological-order definition of def \ref{def-topological-order}, and the node-splitting construction of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}. Let
    \[ W \ins V \]
    be a subset of the output-node set of $G$, and let
    \[ G_{\spl(W)} \;=\; \lp J_{\spl(W)}, V_{\spl(W)}, E_{\spl(W)}, L_{\spl(W)} \rp \]
    be the node-split graph of $G$ w.r.t.\ $W$ as constructed in def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}. Both quantifiers ``for every CADMG $G$'' and ``for every $W \ins V$'' are read \emph{universally}, with the side condition $W \ins V$ inherited verbatim from def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}; the case $W = \emptyset$ is admitted (then $W^0 = W^1 = \emptyset$ and $G_{\spl(W)} = G$ by items i.--iv.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}).

    \emph{Carrier of $G_{\spl(W)}$.} By items i.\ and ii.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting},
    \[
        J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)} \;=\; J \cup (V \sm W) \dcup W^0 \dcup W^1,
    \]
    where $W^0 := \lC w^0 \st w \in W \rC$ and $W^1 := \lC w^1 \st w \in W \rC$ are the two tagged copies of $W$ introduced in def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}. The four constituents $J$, $V \sm W$, $W^0$, $W^1$ are pairwise type-level disjoint by def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}: $J \cap V = \emptyset$ (def \ref{def-cdmg}), $(V \sm W) \cap W = \emptyset$, and the tagged copies $W^0$, $W^1$ are formally distinct from each element of $J \cup V$ and from each other ($W^0 \cap W^1 = \emptyset$, $w^0 \neq w^1$ for every $w \in W$).

    \emph{Finiteness and indexing of $J \cup V$.} By def \ref{def-cdmg} items~i.--ii., the set $J \cup V$ is finite; set
    \[ n \;:=\; |J \cup V|. \]

    \emph{The remark.} The following conjunction of sub-claims holds.

    \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
        \item \emph{$G_{\spl(W)}$ is acyclic.}
              The CDMG $G_{\spl(W)}$ is acyclic in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic}, i.e.\ for every $x \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)} = J \cup (V \sm W) \dcup W^0 \dcup W^1$, there does not exist any non-trivial directed walk from $x$ to itself in $G_{\spl(W)}$.

        \item \emph{An explicit topological order on $G_{\spl(W)}$ obtained from any topological order on $G$.}
              For every topological order $<$ of $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order}, presented in the equivalent indexing form (def \ref{def-topological-order}, ``Equivalent indexing form'') as a bijection
              \[
                  \{1, 2, \dots, n\} \;\longrightarrow\; J \cup V, \qquad i \;\longmapsto\; v_i,
              \]
              with $v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_n$ (so that ``parents precede children'' holds in this enumeration: $v_i \in \Pa^G(v_j) \Rightarrow i < j$), the binary relation $<_{\spl}$ on $J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$ defined below is a topological order of $G_{\spl(W)}$ in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order}.

              \emph{Index map.}
              Define the index map
              \[ \phi \,:\, J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)} \;\longrightarrow\; \Q \]
              by case analysis on the disjoint-union carrier $J \cup (V \sm W) \dcup W^0 \dcup W^1$:
              \begin{itemize}
                  \item \emph{Unsplit nodes retain their original index.}
                        For every $x \in J \cup (V \sm W)$, write $x = v_j$ with $j \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$ the unique index given by the enumeration of $J \cup V$ above, and put
                        \[ \phi(x) \;:=\; j. \]
                  \item \emph{Tagged copies of $W$ are placed symmetrically around the original index.}
                        For every $w \in W$, write $w = v_j$ with $j \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$ the unique index given by the enumeration of $J \cup V$ above, and put
                        \[
                            \phi(w^0) \;:=\; j - \tfrac{1}{3}, \qquad \phi(w^1) \;:=\; j + \tfrac{1}{3}.
                        \]
              \end{itemize}

              \emph{Order from index map.}
              Define
              \[
                  x \;<_{\spl}\; y \quad :\Longleftrightarrow\quad \phi(x) \,<\, \phi(y) \qquad \text{for } x, y \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)},
              \]
              where the right-hand inequality is the standard strict order on $\Q$.
    \end{enumerate}

    \emph{Where each clause of the LN's prose lands.}
    The LN's clauses ``$v_j^0$ the index $j - \tfrac{1}{3}$'' and ``$v_j^1$ the index $j + \tfrac{1}{3}$'' for $v_j \in W$ are the second bullet of the index map above. The LN's closing prose ``and then ordering all nodes according to their index value'' is the definition of $<_{\spl}$ from $\phi$ via the strict order on $\Q$. The first bullet of the index map -- ``unsplit nodes $v_j \in J \cup (V \sm W)$ retain index $j$'' -- is the rule the LN leaves implicit: the LN's literal text assigns indices only to the tagged copies $v_j^0$, $v_j^1$ of split nodes $v_j \in W$ and says nothing about the indices of $v_j \in J \cup (V \sm W)$, but the closing instruction ``order all nodes according to their index value'' is well-defined on the carrier $J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$ only if the unsplit nodes also carry an index. The natural and only-consistent reading is that the original index $j$ is retained for unsplit nodes; the first bullet records this retention rule explicitly.

    \emph{Compatibility of the tagging convention with def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}.}
    For every $w \in W$ with $w = v_j$, $\phi(w^0) = j - \tfrac{1}{3} < j + \tfrac{1}{3} = \phi(w^1)$, so $w^0 <_{\spl} w^1$. This is consistent with item iii.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}: that item adds the \emph{transfer} edge $(w^0, w^1) \in E_{\spl(W)}$ for every $w \in W$, i.e.\ $w^0 \in \Pa^{G_{\spl(W)}}(w^1)$ in the sense of def \ref{def_3_5}, item~i., and the parent-precedence clause of def \ref{def-topological-order} for $G_{\spl(W)}$ then demands precisely $w^0 <_{\spl} w^1$. (Under the opposite tagging convention -- transfer edge $(w^1, w^0)$, incoming edges into $w$ reattached at $w^1$, outgoing edges out of $w$ leaving $w^0$ -- the symmetric index assignment ``$j - \tfrac{1}{3}$ for the $^0$-copy and $j + \tfrac{1}{3}$ for the $^1$-copy'' would not produce a topological order; sub-claim~(b) is correct as stated precisely because the index choice matches def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}'s tagging convention.)

    \emph{Role of the CADMG (acyclicity) precondition.}
    The construction of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting} on its own does not preserve acyclicity from $G$ to $G_{\spl(W)}$: if $(w, w) \in E$ for some $w \in W$, then the first set-builder in item iii.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting} adds the lifted directed edge $(w^1, w^0) \in E_{\spl(W)}$, which together with the transfer edge $(w^0, w^1) \in E_{\spl(W)}$ realises a length-$2$ directed cycle $w^0 \to w^1 \to w^0$ in $G_{\spl(W)}$ (cf.\ the closing paragraph ``Self-loops on $W$ produce $2$-cycles, and $G_{\spl(W)}$ is still a CDMG'' of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}). The CADMG hypothesis on $G$ -- i.e.\ acyclicity of $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic} -- rules this hazard out: by the consequence ``no directed self-loops on output nodes'' established in def \ref{def-acylic}, an acyclic CDMG has $(v, v) \notin E$ for every $v \in V$, and in particular $(w, w) \notin E$ for every $w \in W \ins V$. Thus the directed-edge set $E_{\spl(W)}$ is free of the $2$-cycle hazard of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}, which is what allows sub-claim~(a) to hold. The bidirected-edge set $L$ plays no role in either sub-claim: acyclicity (def \ref{def-acylic}) is defined via the non-existence of non-trivial directed walks (which use only $E$), and a topological order (def \ref{def-topological-order}) is defined via the parent-set $\Pa^G$ (def \ref{def_3_5}, item~i., which is defined from $E$ alone).
\end{Rem}

\end{document}

    For every $w \in W$ with $w = v_j$, $\phi(w^0) = j - \tfrac{1}{3} < j + \tfrac{1}{3} = \phi(w^1)$, so $w^0 <_{\spl} w^1$. (Under the opposite tagging convention -- transfer edge $(w^1, w^0)$, incoming edges into $w$ reattached at $w^1$, outgoing edges out of $w$ leaving $w^0$ -- the symmetric index assignment ``$j - \tfrac{1}{3}$ for the $^0$-copy and $j + \tfrac{1}{3}$ for the $^1$-copy'' would not produce a topological order; sub-claim~(b) is correct as stated precisely because the index choice matches def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}'s tagging convention.)

    \emph{Role of the CADMG (acyclicity) precondition.}
    The construction of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting} on its own does not preserve acyclicity from $G$ to $G_{\spl(W)}$: if $(w, w) \in E$ for some $w \in W$, then the first set-builder in item iii.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting} adds the lifted directed edge $(w^1, w^0) \in E_{\spl(W)}$, which together with the transfer edge $(w^0, w^1) \in E_{\spl(W)}$ realises a length-$2$ directed cycle $w^0 \to w^1 \to w^0$ in $G_{\spl(W)}$ (cf.\ the closing paragraph ``Self-loops on $W$ produce $2$-cycles, and $G_{\spl(W)}$ is still a CDMG'' of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}). The CADMG hypothesis on $G$ -- i.e.\ acyclicity of $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic} -- rules this hazard out: by the consequence ``no directed self-loops on output nodes'' established in def \ref{def-acylic}, an acyclic CDMG has $(v, v) \notin E$ for every $v \in V$, and in particular $(w, w) \notin E$ for every $w \in W \ins V$. Thus the directed-edge set $E_{\spl(W)}$ is free of the $2$-cycle hazard of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}, which is what allows sub-claim~(a) to hold. The bidirected-edge set $L$ plays no role in either sub-claim: acyclicity (def \ref{def-acylic}) is defined via the non-existence of non-trivial directed walks (which use only $E$), and a topological order (def \ref{def-topological-order}) is defined via the parent-set $\Pa^G$ (def \ref{def_3_5}, item~i., which is defined from $E$ alone).
\end{Rem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{proof}
% The LN's claim_3_6 block in lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex:444--455
% is a \begin{Rem} that only describes the construction (the $j \pm 1/3$
% index map) and does NOT contain a \begin{proof} block. We therefore
% construct the proof from scratch, in the LN paradigm.
%
% Strategy: prove (b) first via two key lemmas about the index map $\phi$
% -- (i) $\phi$ is injective, (ii) $\phi$ is strictly increasing along
% every directed edge of $G_{\spl(W)}$ -- and then derive (a) as an
% immediate corollary of (ii) via the standard cycle-vs-strict-increase
% contradiction.

We prove the two sub-claims in the order (b), then (a). Sub-claim~(a) is derived from the same key lemma about the index map $\phi$ that drives sub-claim~(b): that $\phi$ is strictly increasing along every directed edge of $G_{\spl(W)}$. Throughout the proof we use the explicit form of the carrier and edge set of $G_{\spl(W)}$ given in def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}, namely
\[
    J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)} \;=\; J \cup (V \sm W) \dcup W^0 \dcup W^1
\]
(items i.\ and ii.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}, type-level disjoint by the tagged construction recalled in the statement block above), and
\[
    E_{\spl(W)} \;=\; \lC (v_1^1, v_2^0) \st (v_1, v_2) \in E \rC \;\cup\; \lC (w^0, w^1) \st w \in W \rC
\]
(item iii.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}, the two set-builders being respectively the \emph{lifted} and the \emph{transfer} edges). The bidirected-edge set $L_{\spl(W)}$ plays no role in either sub-claim, for the reason recorded in the closing paragraph of the statement block above (acyclicity, def \ref{def-acylic}, and topological orders, def \ref{def-topological-order}, are both defined from $E$ alone).

Fix once and for all an enumeration $\{1, 2, \dots, n\} \to J \cup V$, $i \mapsto v_i$, of the carrier of $G$ as in the statement of sub-claim~(b), and let $\phi : J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)} \to \Q$ be the index map defined there. For $x \in J \cup (V \sm W)$, the unique $j \in \{1, \dots, n\}$ with $x = v_j$ exists by bijectivity of the enumeration; for $w \in W$, the unique $j \in \{1, \dots, n\}$ with $w = v_j$ exists for the same reason. Hence $\phi$ is well-defined on the disjoint-union carrier $J \cup (V \sm W) \dcup W^0 \dcup W^1$ by the two-bullet case analysis: every $x \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$ lies in exactly one of the four constituents and the corresponding bullet supplies $\phi(x)$ unambiguously.

\medskip\noindent\emph{(0) Lemma: $\phi$ is injective.}
Let $x, y \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$ with $\phi(x) = \phi(y)$; we show $x = y$ by case analysis on which of the four constituents each of $x$ and $y$ lies in.

By the definition of $\phi$ via the two bullets and the disjointness of $J \cup (V \sm W)$, $W^0$, $W^1$, the value $\phi(x)$ lies in:
\begin{itemize}
    \item $\{1, 2, \dots, n\} \subseteq \Z$ if $x \in J \cup (V \sm W)$ (first bullet);
    \item $\{j - \tfrac{1}{3} : j \in \{1, \dots, n\}\} \subseteq \Z - \tfrac{1}{3}$ if $x \in W^0$ (second bullet, $^0$-half);
    \item $\{j + \tfrac{1}{3} : j \in \{1, \dots, n\}\} \subseteq \Z + \tfrac{1}{3}$ if $x \in W^1$ (second bullet, $^1$-half);
\end{itemize}
and analogously for $\phi(y)$. The three target sets are pairwise disjoint in $\Q$: an integer $j \in \Z$ is never of the form $k - \tfrac{1}{3}$ or $k + \tfrac{1}{3}$ for any integer $k$ (since $\tfrac{1}{3} \notin \Z$), and $j - \tfrac{1}{3} = k + \tfrac{1}{3}$ would force $j - k = \tfrac{2}{3}$, impossible for $j, k \in \Z$. Hence $\phi(x) = \phi(y)$ already forces $x$ and $y$ to lie in the \emph{same} constituent. We consider the three possible such constituents.

\emph{Case 1: $x, y \in J \cup (V \sm W)$.} Write $x = v_{j_x}$ and $y = v_{j_y}$ with $j_x, j_y \in \{1, \dots, n\}$. Then $\phi(x) = j_x$ and $\phi(y) = j_y$ by the first bullet, so $\phi(x) = \phi(y)$ gives $j_x = j_y$, and bijectivity of the enumeration $i \mapsto v_i$ then gives $v_{j_x} = v_{j_y}$, i.e.\ $x = y$.

\emph{Case 2: $x, y \in W^0$.} Write $x = w_x^0$ and $y = w_y^0$ with $w_x, w_y \in W$, and let $j_x, j_y \in \{1, \dots, n\}$ be such that $w_x = v_{j_x}$ and $w_y = v_{j_y}$. Then $\phi(x) = j_x - \tfrac{1}{3}$ and $\phi(y) = j_y - \tfrac{1}{3}$ by the second bullet, so $\phi(x) = \phi(y)$ gives $j_x = j_y$, hence $w_x = v_{j_x} = v_{j_y} = w_y$, and finally $x = w_x^0 = w_y^0 = y$ (injectivity of $w \mapsto w^0$, def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}).

\emph{Case 3: $x, y \in W^1$.} Symmetric to Case~2, with $w^1$ in place of $w^0$ and $j + \tfrac{1}{3}$ in place of $j - \tfrac{1}{3}$ throughout.

In each case $x = y$, completing the proof of injectivity of $\phi$.

\medskip\noindent\emph{(1) Lemma: $\phi$ is strictly increasing along every directed edge of $G_{\spl(W)}$.}
We show that for every $(x, y) \in E_{\spl(W)}$,
\[
    \phi(x) \;<\; \phi(y).
\]
By the explicit form of $E_{\spl(W)}$ recalled above (item iii.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}), every $(x, y) \in E_{\spl(W)}$ is either a \emph{lifted} edge $(x, y) = (v_1^1, v_2^0)$ arising from some $(v_1, v_2) \in E$, or a \emph{transfer} edge $(x, y) = (w^0, w^1)$ arising from some $w \in W$. We handle the two cases.

\emph{Case T (transfer edge).} If $(x, y) = (w^0, w^1)$ for some $w \in W$, write $w = v_j$ with $j \in \{1, \dots, n\}$. By the second bullet of the index map,
\[
    \phi(x) \;=\; \phi(w^0) \;=\; j - \tfrac{1}{3} \;<\; j + \tfrac{1}{3} \;=\; \phi(w^1) \;=\; \phi(y),
\]
using $-\tfrac{1}{3} < +\tfrac{1}{3}$ in $\Q$. (This case does not use the topological-order hypothesis on $<$ at all; the transfer edge is hard-wired into the construction of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}, and the symmetric placement $j \pm \tfrac{1}{3}$ of the two tagged copies around the original index $j$ encodes its direction.)

\emph{Case L (lifted edge).} If $(x, y) = (v_1^1, v_2^0)$ for some $(v_1, v_2) \in E$, write $v_1 = v_{j_1}$ and $v_2 = v_{j_2}$ with $j_1, j_2 \in \{1, \dots, n\}$ (using the typing $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$ of def \ref{def-cdmg}, item~iii., so that both endpoints lie in $J \cup V$ and the enumeration applies to each).

Since $(v_1, v_2) \in E$, the set-builder definition of the parent set in $G$ (def \ref{def_3_5}, item~i.) gives $v_1 \in \Pa^G(v_2)$. The hypothesis that $<$ is a topological order of $G$ (def \ref{def-topological-order}), unwound in its equivalent indexing form (def \ref{def-topological-order}, ``Equivalent indexing form''), then yields
\[
    j_1 \;<\; j_2 \qquad \text{in } \{1, 2, \dots, n\} \ins \Z.
\]
Since $j_1, j_2$ are integers, $j_1 < j_2$ implies $j_2 - j_1 \ge 1$.

We now bound $\phi(v_1^1)$ from above and $\phi(v_2^0)$ from below by case analysis on the membership of $v_1$ and $v_2$ in $W$, using the shorthand-and-tag convention of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting} (recalled in the lifted-edge bullets of item iii.\ of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}):
\begin{itemize}
    \item For the \emph{source} $v_1^1$: if $v_1 \in W$, then $v_1^1 \in W^1$ is the tagged copy and $\phi(v_1^1) = j_1 + \tfrac{1}{3}$ by the second bullet of the index map; if $v_1 \in J \cup (V \sm W)$, then $v_1^1 = v_1$ (the shorthand for unsplit nodes, def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}) and $\phi(v_1^1) = \phi(v_1) = j_1$ by the first bullet. In either subcase,
        \[
            \phi(v_1^1) \;\le\; j_1 + \tfrac{1}{3}.
        \]
    \item For the \emph{target} $v_2^0$: by def \ref{def-cdmg}, item~iii., the second endpoint of every directed edge in $E$ lies in $V$, so $v_2 \in V$, and the dichotomy $v_2 \in W$ vs.\ $v_2 \in V \sm W$ is exhaustive. If $v_2 \in W$, then $v_2^0 \in W^0$ is the tagged copy and $\phi(v_2^0) = j_2 - \tfrac{1}{3}$ by the second bullet; if $v_2 \in V \sm W$, then $v_2^0 = v_2$ (the shorthand for unsplit nodes) and $\phi(v_2^0) = \phi(v_2) = j_2$ by the first bullet. In either subcase,
        \[
            \phi(v_2^0) \;\ge\; j_2 - \tfrac{1}{3}.
        \]
\end{itemize}

Combining the two displayed bounds with $j_2 - j_1 \ge 1$,
\[
    \phi(v_1^1) \;\le\; j_1 + \tfrac{1}{3}
                \;=\; j_2 - \bigl( (j_2 - j_1) - \tfrac{1}{3} \bigr)
                \;\le\; j_2 - \bigl( 1 - \tfrac{1}{3} \bigr)
                \;=\; j_2 - \tfrac{2}{3}
                \;<\; j_2 - \tfrac{1}{3}
                \;\le\; \phi(v_2^0),
\]
where the strict inequality $j_2 - \tfrac{2}{3} < j_2 - \tfrac{1}{3}$ is $-\tfrac{2}{3} < -\tfrac{1}{3}$ in $\Q$. Hence $\phi(x) = \phi(v_1^1) < \phi(v_2^0) = \phi(y)$.

In both cases T and L, $\phi(x) < \phi(y)$, completing the proof of Lemma~(1).

\medskip\noindent\emph{Proof of sub-claim (b): $<_{\spl}$ is a topological order of $G_{\spl(W)}$.}

By def \ref{def-topological-order}, we must verify that $<_{\spl}$ is a strict total order on $J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$ -- i.e.\ irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous on that carrier -- and that the parent-precedence clause
\[
    \forall\, x, y \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)} :\quad x \in \Pa^{G_{\spl(W)}}(y) \;\Longrightarrow\; x <_{\spl} y
\]
holds.

\emph{Strict total order on $J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$.} By definition, $x <_{\spl} y \Leftrightarrow \phi(x) < \phi(y)$, with the right-hand $<$ the standard strict order on $\Q$. The standard strict order on $\Q$ is irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous on $\Q$; we transfer each property to $<_{\spl}$ on $J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$ via the map $\phi : J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)} \to \Q$.
\begin{itemize}
    \item \emph{Irreflexivity.} For every $x \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$, $\phi(x) < \phi(x)$ is false in $\Q$ (irreflexivity of $<$ on $\Q$). Hence $x <_{\spl} x$ is false.
    \item \emph{Transitivity.} For $x, y, z \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$ with $x <_{\spl} y$ and $y <_{\spl} z$, we have $\phi(x) < \phi(y)$ and $\phi(y) < \phi(z)$ in $\Q$, hence $\phi(x) < \phi(z)$ by transitivity of $<$ on $\Q$, i.e.\ $x <_{\spl} z$.
    \item \emph{Trichotomy.} For $x, y \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$, trichotomy of $<$ on $\Q$ applied to $\phi(x), \phi(y)$ gives one of the three alternatives
        \[
            \phi(x) < \phi(y), \quad \phi(x) = \phi(y), \quad \phi(y) < \phi(x).
        \]
        The middle alternative $\phi(x) = \phi(y)$ implies $x = y$ by Lemma~(0). The other two alternatives say $x <_{\spl} y$ and $y <_{\spl} x$ respectively. Hence one of $x <_{\spl} y$, $x = y$, $y <_{\spl} x$ holds for every pair $x, y \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$.
\end{itemize}
This verifies that $<_{\spl}$ is a strict total order on $J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$.

\emph{Parent-precedence.} Let $x, y \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$ with $x \in \Pa^{G_{\spl(W)}}(y)$. By the set-builder definition of the parent set (def \ref{def_3_5}, item~i.) applied to the CDMG $G_{\spl(W)}$,
\[
    \Pa^{G_{\spl(W)}}(y) \;=\; \lC u \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)} \,\big|\, (u, y) \in E_{\spl(W)} \rC,
\]
so $(x, y) \in E_{\spl(W)}$. By Lemma~(1), $\phi(x) < \phi(y)$, i.e.\ $x <_{\spl} y$.

Both conjuncts of def \ref{def-topological-order} are verified for $<_{\spl}$ on the CDMG $G_{\spl(W)}$, so $<_{\spl}$ is a topological order of $G_{\spl(W)}$, establishing sub-claim~(b).

\medskip\noindent\emph{Proof of sub-claim (a): $G_{\spl(W)}$ is acyclic.}

Sub-claim~(a) makes no reference to any choice of topological order, so the index map $\phi$ used in Lemmas~(0)--(1) above is not provided by the hypotheses of (a) alone. We obtain one as follows. Since $G$ is a CADMG, $G$ is acyclic in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic}; by the $\Rightarrow$ direction of \refrow{claim_3_2} applied to $G$, there exists at least one topological order of $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def-topological-order}. Fix any such order $<$ and present it in its equivalent indexing form (def \ref{def-topological-order}, ``Equivalent indexing form'') as a bijection $\{1, 2, \dots, n\} \to J \cup V$, $i \mapsto v_i$, with $v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_n$. With this $<$ and this enumeration in hand, build the index map $\phi : J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)} \to \Q$ exactly as in the statement of sub-claim~(b), and apply Lemma~(1) (whose statement and proof only require that $<$ is some topological order of $G$): for every $(x, y) \in E_{\spl(W)}$,
\[
    \phi(x) \;<\; \phi(y).
\]

We now argue by contradiction. Suppose, for contradiction, that $G_{\spl(W)}$ admits a non-trivial directed walk from some node to itself, i.e.\ (in the sense of def \ref{def:walks}, item~ii., and def \ref{def-acylic}) a tuple
\[
    p \;=\; (x_0, a_0, x_1, a_1, \dots, a_{k-1}, x_k)
\]
with $k \ge 1$, $x_0 = x_k \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$, each $x_i \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$, and each $a_i = (x_i, x_{i+1}) \in E_{\spl(W)}$.

By Lemma~(1) applied to each edge $a_i = (x_i, x_{i+1}) \in E_{\spl(W)}$,
\[
    \phi(x_0) \;<\; \phi(x_1) \;<\; \phi(x_2) \;<\; \cdots \;<\; \phi(x_k),
\]
hence $\phi(x_0) < \phi(x_k)$ by $k \ge 1$ applications of strict transitivity of $<$ on $\Q$ (the chain is non-degenerate since $k \ge 1$ supplies at least one strict step). But $x_0 = x_k$, so $\phi(x_0) = \phi(x_k)$, contradicting the strict inequality $\phi(x_0) < \phi(x_k)$ via irreflexivity of $<$ on $\Q$.

No such walk $p$ can exist, so $G_{\spl(W)}$ is acyclic in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic}, establishing sub-claim~(a).

\medskip\noindent\emph{Remark on an alternative derivation of (a) as a corollary of (b).}
Once sub-claim~(b) is established, sub-claim~(a) admits a one-line derivation: pick any topological order $<$ of $G$ (which exists by the $\Rightarrow$ direction of \refrow{claim_3_2}, since $G$ is acyclic by the CADMG hypothesis); by (b), $<_{\spl}$ is a topological order of $G_{\spl(W)}$; and then the $\Leftarrow$ direction of \refrow{claim_3_2} applied to the CDMG $G_{\spl(W)}$ (which is a CDMG by the construction and consistency check of def \ref{def:G_node-splitting}) yields acyclicity of $G_{\spl(W)}$. Our direct proof of (a) above is the unwinding of this corollary at the level of the index map $\phi$: it makes the chain $\phi(x_0) < \cdots < \phi(x_k)$ explicit instead of bundling it inside the proof of \refrow{claim_3_2}. The two arguments are equivalent; we have presented the direct version because it makes the index-map $\phi$ the single shared mechanism behind both sub-claims.

\medskip\noindent\emph{Closing remark on the role of the CADMG hypothesis.}
The CADMG hypothesis on $G$ -- i.e.\ acyclicity of $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def-acylic} -- enters the proof of (a) as the precondition for invoking the $\Rightarrow$ direction of \refrow{claim_3_2} to obtain a topological order $<$ of $G$; sub-claim~(b), in contrast, only uses that the supplied $<$ \emph{is} a topological order, not the acyclicity hypothesis on $G$ directly. The structural hazard flagged in the closing paragraph of the statement block above -- a self-loop $(w, w) \in E$ on some $w \in W$ producing the $2$-cycle $w^0 \to w^1 \to w^0$ in $G_{\spl(W)}$ -- is ruled out by the same hypothesis: the ``no directed self-loops on output nodes'' consequence of def \ref{def-acylic} gives $(w, w) \notin E$ for every $w \in W \ins V$, so the lifted edge $(w^1, w^0)$ never enters $E_{\spl(W)}$. Consistently with this, were $(w, w) \in E$ to hold for some $w \in W$, Case~L of Lemma~(1) applied to the lifted edge $(w^1, w^0)$ would demand $j_1 < j_2$ for $j_1 = j_2 = j$ (the index of $w = v_j$), which is false; the CADMG hypothesis is precisely what prevents this incoherence from arising upstream.
\end{proof}

\end{document}

Remark — SplitTopologicalOrder

Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CADMG, i.e.\ a CDMG (def def-cdmg) that is acyclic in the sense of def def-acylic (equivalently, $G$ satisfies the attribute “CADMG” of def def-cdmg-types, item i.); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg, the family-relationship notation of def def_3_5, the topological-order definition of def def-topological-order, and the node-splitting construction of def def:G_node-splitting. Let \[ W \ins V \] be a subset of the output-node set of $G$, and let \[ G_{\spl(W)} \;=\; \lp J_{\spl(W)}, V_{\spl(W)}, E_{\spl(W)}, L_{\spl(W)} \rp \] be the node-split graph of $G$ w.r.t.\ $W$ as constructed in def def:G_node-splitting. Both quantifiers “for every CADMG $G$” and “for every $W \ins V$” are read universally, with the side condition $W \ins V$ inherited verbatim from def def:G_node-splitting; the case $W = \emptyset$ is admitted (then $W^0 = W^1 = \emptyset$ and $G_{\spl(W)} = G$ by items i.–iv.\ of def def:G_node-splitting).

Carrier of $G_{\spl(W)}$. By items i.\ and ii.\ of def def:G_node-splitting, \[ J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)} \;=\; J \cup (V \sm W) \dcup W^0 \dcup W^1, \] where $W^0 := \lC w^0 \st w \in W \rC$ and $W^1 := \lC w^1 \st w \in W \rC$ are the two tagged copies of $W$ introduced in def def:G_node-splitting. The four constituents $J$, $V \sm W$, $W^0$, $W^1$ are pairwise type-level disjoint by def def:G_node-splitting: $J \cap V = \emptyset$ (def def-cdmg), $(V \sm W) \cap W = \emptyset$, and the tagged copies $W^0$, $W^1$ are formally distinct from each element of $J \cup V$ and from each other ($W^0 \cap W^1 = \emptyset$, $w^0 \neq w^1$ for every $w \in W$).

Finiteness and indexing of $J \cup V$. By def def-cdmg items i.–ii., the set $J \cup V$ is finite; set \[ n \;:=\; |J \cup V|. \]

The remark. The following conjunction of sub-claims holds.

  1. $G_{\spl(W)}$ is acyclic. The CDMG $G_{\spl(W)}$ is acyclic in the sense of def def-acylic, i.e.\ for every $x \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)} = J \cup (V \sm W) \dcup W^0 \dcup W^1$, there does not exist any non-trivial directed walk from $x$ to itself in $G_{\spl(W)}$.
  2. An explicit topological order on $G_{\spl(W)}$ obtained from any topological order on $G$. For every topological order $<$ of $G$ in the sense of def def-topological-order, presented in the equivalent indexing form (def def-topological-order, “Equivalent indexing form”) as a bijection \[ \{1, 2, \dots, n\} \;\longrightarrow\; J \cup V, \qquad i \;\longmapsto\; v_i, \] with $v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_n$ (so that “parents precede children” holds in this enumeration: $v_i \in \Pa^G(v_j) \Rightarrow i < j$), the binary relation $<_{\spl}$ on $J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$ defined below is a topological order of $G_{\spl(W)}$ in the sense of def def-topological-order.

    Index map. Define the index map \[ \phi \,:\, J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)} \;\longrightarrow\; \Q \] by case analysis on the disjoint-union carrier $J \cup (V \sm W) \dcup W^0 \dcup W^1$:

    • Unsplit nodes retain their original index. For every $x \in J \cup (V \sm W)$, write $x = v_j$ with $j \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$ the unique index given by the enumeration of $J \cup V$ above, and put \[ \phi(x) \;:=\; j. \]
    • Tagged copies of $W$ are placed symmetrically around the original index. For every $w \in W$, write $w = v_j$ with $j \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$ the unique index given by the enumeration of $J \cup V$ above, and put \[ \phi(w^0) \;:=\; j - \tfrac{1}{3}, \qquad \phi(w^1) \;:=\; j + \tfrac{1}{3}. \]

    Order from index map. Define \[ x \;<_{\spl}\; y \quad :\Longleftrightarrow\quad \phi(x) \,<\, \phi(y) \qquad \text{for } x, y \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}, \] where the right-hand inequality is the standard strict order on $\Q$.

Where each clause of the LN's prose lands. The LN's clauses “$v_j^0$ the index $j - \tfrac{1}{3}$” and “$v_j^1$ the index $j + \tfrac{1}{3}$” for $v_j \in W$ are the second bullet of the index map above. The LN's closing prose “and then ordering all nodes according to their index value” is the definition of $<_{\spl}$ from $\phi$ via the strict order on $\Q$. The first bullet of the index map – “unsplit nodes $v_j \in J \cup (V \sm W)$ retain index $j$” – is the rule the LN leaves implicit: the LN's literal text assigns indices only to the tagged copies $v_j^0$, $v_j^1$ of split nodes $v_j \in W$ and says nothing about the indices of $v_j \in J \cup (V \sm W)$, but the closing instruction “order all nodes according to their index value” is well-defined on the carrier $J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$ only if the unsplit nodes also carry an index. The natural and only-consistent reading is that the original index $j$ is retained for unsplit nodes; the first bullet records this retention rule explicitly.

Compatibility of the tagging convention with def def:G_node-splitting. For every $w \in W$ with $w = v_j$, $\phi(w^0) = j - \tfrac{1}{3} < j + \tfrac{1}{3} = \phi(w^1)$, so $w^0 <_{\spl} w^1$. This is consistent with item iii.\ of def def:G_node-splitting: that item adds the transfer edge $(w^0, w^1) \in E_{\spl(W)}$ for every $w \in W$, i.e.\ $w^0 \in \Pa^{G_{\spl(W)}}(w^1)$ in the sense of def def_3_5, item i., and the parent-precedence clause of def def-topological-order for $G_{\spl(W)}$ then demands precisely $w^0 <_{\spl} w^1$. (Under the opposite tagging convention – transfer edge $(w^1, w^0)$, incoming edges into $w$ reattached at $w^1$, outgoing edges out of $w$ leaving $w^0$ – the symmetric index assignment “$j - \tfrac{1}{3}$ for the $^0$-copy and $j + \tfrac{1}{3}$ for the $^1$-copy” would not produce a topological order; sub-claim (b) is correct as stated precisely because the index choice matches def def:G_node-splitting's tagging convention.)

Role of the CADMG (acyclicity) precondition. The construction of def def:G_node-splitting on its own does not preserve acyclicity from $G$ to $G_{\spl(W)}$: if $(w, w) \in E$ for some $w \in W$, then the first set-builder in item iii.\ of def def:G_node-splitting adds the lifted directed edge $(w^1, w^0) \in E_{\spl(W)}$, which together with the transfer edge $(w^0, w^1) \in E_{\spl(W)}$ realises a length-$2$ directed cycle $w^0 \to w^1 \to w^0$ in $G_{\spl(W)}$ (cf.\ the closing paragraph “Self-loops on $W$ produce $2$-cycles, and $G_{\spl(W)}$ is still a CDMG” of def def:G_node-splitting). The CADMG hypothesis on $G$ – i.e.\ acyclicity of $G$ in the sense of def def-acylic – rules this hazard out: by the consequence “no directed self-loops on output nodes” established in def def-acylic, an acyclic CDMG has $(v, v) \notin E$ for every $v \in V$, and in particular $(w, w) \notin E$ for every $w \in W \ins V$. Thus the directed-edge set $E_{\spl(W)}$ is free of the $2$-cycle hazard of def def:G_node-splitting, which is what allows sub-claim (a) to hold. The bidirected-edge set $L$ plays no role in either sub-claim: acyclicity (def def-acylic) is defined via the non-existence of non-trivial directed walks (which use only $E$), and a topological order (def def-topological-order) is defined via the parent-set $\Pa^G$ (def def_3_5, item i., which is defined from $E$ alone).

Proof.

We prove the two sub-claims in the order (b), then (a). Sub-claim (a) is derived from the same key lemma about the index map $\phi$ that drives sub-claim (b): that $\phi$ is strictly increasing along every directed edge of $G_{\spl(W)}$. Throughout the proof we use the explicit form of the carrier and edge set of $G_{\spl(W)}$ given in def def:G_node-splitting, namely \[ J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)} \;=\; J \cup (V \sm W) \dcup W^0 \dcup W^1 \] (items i.\ and ii.\ of def def:G_node-splitting, type-level disjoint by the tagged construction recalled in the statement block above), and \[ E_{\spl(W)} \;=\; \lC (v_1^1, v_2^0) \st (v_1, v_2) \in E \rC \;\cup\; \lC (w^0, w^1) \st w \in W \rC \] (item iii.\ of def def:G_node-splitting, the two set-builders being respectively the lifted and the transfer edges). The bidirected-edge set $L_{\spl(W)}$ plays no role in either sub-claim, for the reason recorded in the closing paragraph of the statement block above (acyclicity, def def-acylic, and topological orders, def def-topological-order, are both defined from $E$ alone).

Fix once and for all an enumeration $\{1, 2, \dots, n\} \to J \cup V$, $i \mapsto v_i$, of the carrier of $G$ as in the statement of sub-claim (b), and let $\phi : J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)} \to \Q$ be the index map defined there. For $x \in J \cup (V \sm W)$, the unique $j \in \{1, \dots, n\}$ with $x = v_j$ exists by bijectivity of the enumeration; for $w \in W$, the unique $j \in \{1, \dots, n\}$ with $w = v_j$ exists for the same reason. Hence $\phi$ is well-defined on the disjoint-union carrier $J \cup (V \sm W) \dcup W^0 \dcup W^1$ by the two-bullet case analysis: every $x \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$ lies in exactly one of the four constituents and the corresponding bullet supplies $\phi(x)$ unambiguously.

(0) Lemma: $\phi$ is injective. Let $x, y \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$ with $\phi(x) = \phi(y)$; we show $x = y$ by case analysis on which of the four constituents each of $x$ and $y$ lies in.

By the definition of $\phi$ via the two bullets and the disjointness of $J \cup (V \sm W)$, $W^0$, $W^1$, the value $\phi(x)$ lies in:

  • $\{1, 2, \dots, n\} \subseteq \Z$ if $x \in J \cup (V \sm W)$ (first bullet);
  • $\{j - \tfrac{1}{3} : j \in \{1, \dots, n\}\} \subseteq \Z - \tfrac{1}{3}$ if $x \in W^0$ (second bullet, $^0$-half);
  • $\{j + \tfrac{1}{3} : j \in \{1, \dots, n\}\} \subseteq \Z + \tfrac{1}{3}$ if $x \in W^1$ (second bullet, $^1$-half);

and analogously for $\phi(y)$. The three target sets are pairwise disjoint in $\Q$: an integer $j \in \Z$ is never of the form $k - \tfrac{1}{3}$ or $k + \tfrac{1}{3}$ for any integer $k$ (since $\tfrac{1}{3} \notin \Z$), and $j - \tfrac{1}{3} = k + \tfrac{1}{3}$ would force $j - k = \tfrac{2}{3}$, impossible for $j, k \in \Z$. Hence $\phi(x) = \phi(y)$ already forces $x$ and $y$ to lie in the same constituent. We consider the three possible such constituents.

Case 1: $x, y \in J \cup (V \sm W)$. Write $x = v_{j_x}$ and $y = v_{j_y}$ with $j_x, j_y \in \{1, \dots, n\}$. Then $\phi(x) = j_x$ and $\phi(y) = j_y$ by the first bullet, so $\phi(x) = \phi(y)$ gives $j_x = j_y$, and bijectivity of the enumeration $i \mapsto v_i$ then gives $v_{j_x} = v_{j_y}$, i.e.\ $x = y$.

Case 2: $x, y \in W^0$. Write $x = w_x^0$ and $y = w_y^0$ with $w_x, w_y \in W$, and let $j_x, j_y \in \{1, \dots, n\}$ be such that $w_x = v_{j_x}$ and $w_y = v_{j_y}$. Then $\phi(x) = j_x - \tfrac{1}{3}$ and $\phi(y) = j_y - \tfrac{1}{3}$ by the second bullet, so $\phi(x) = \phi(y)$ gives $j_x = j_y$, hence $w_x = v_{j_x} = v_{j_y} = w_y$, and finally $x = w_x^0 = w_y^0 = y$ (injectivity of $w \mapsto w^0$, def def:G_node-splitting).

Case 3: $x, y \in W^1$. Symmetric to Case 2, with $w^1$ in place of $w^0$ and $j + \tfrac{1}{3}$ in place of $j - \tfrac{1}{3}$ throughout.

In each case $x = y$, completing the proof of injectivity of $\phi$.

(1) Lemma: $\phi$ is strictly increasing along every directed edge of $G_{\spl(W)}$. We show that for every $(x, y) \in E_{\spl(W)}$, \[ \phi(x) \;<\; \phi(y). \] By the explicit form of $E_{\spl(W)}$ recalled above (item iii.\ of def def:G_node-splitting), every $(x, y) \in E_{\spl(W)}$ is either a lifted edge $(x, y) = (v_1^1, v_2^0)$ arising from some $(v_1, v_2) \in E$, or a transfer edge $(x, y) = (w^0, w^1)$ arising from some $w \in W$. We handle the two cases.

Case T (transfer edge). If $(x, y) = (w^0, w^1)$ for some $w \in W$, write $w = v_j$ with $j \in \{1, \dots, n\}$. By the second bullet of the index map, \[ \phi(x) \;=\; \phi(w^0) \;=\; j - \tfrac{1}{3} \;<\; j + \tfrac{1}{3} \;=\; \phi(w^1) \;=\; \phi(y), \] using $-\tfrac{1}{3} < +\tfrac{1}{3}$ in $\Q$. (This case does not use the topological-order hypothesis on $<$ at all; the transfer edge is hard-wired into the construction of def def:G_node-splitting, and the symmetric placement $j \pm \tfrac{1}{3}$ of the two tagged copies around the original index $j$ encodes its direction.)

Case L (lifted edge). If $(x, y) = (v_1^1, v_2^0)$ for some $(v_1, v_2) \in E$, write $v_1 = v_{j_1}$ and $v_2 = v_{j_2}$ with $j_1, j_2 \in \{1, \dots, n\}$ (using the typing $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$ of def def-cdmg, item iii., so that both endpoints lie in $J \cup V$ and the enumeration applies to each).

Since $(v_1, v_2) \in E$, the set-builder definition of the parent set in $G$ (def def_3_5, item i.) gives $v_1 \in \Pa^G(v_2)$. The hypothesis that $<$ is a topological order of $G$ (def def-topological-order), unwound in its equivalent indexing form (def def-topological-order, “Equivalent indexing form”), then yields \[ j_1 \;<\; j_2 \qquad \text{in } \{1, 2, \dots, n\} \ins \Z. \] Since $j_1, j_2$ are integers, $j_1 < j_2$ implies $j_2 - j_1 \ge 1$.

We now bound $\phi(v_1^1)$ from above and $\phi(v_2^0)$ from below by case analysis on the membership of $v_1$ and $v_2$ in $W$, using the shorthand-and-tag convention of def def:G_node-splitting (recalled in the lifted-edge bullets of item iii.\ of def def:G_node-splitting):

  • For the source $v_1^1$: if $v_1 \in W$, then $v_1^1 \in W^1$ is the tagged copy and $\phi(v_1^1) = j_1 + \tfrac{1}{3}$ by the second bullet of the index map; if $v_1 \in J \cup (V \sm W)$, then $v_1^1 = v_1$ (the shorthand for unsplit nodes, def def:G_node-splitting) and $\phi(v_1^1) = \phi(v_1) = j_1$ by the first bullet. In either subcase, \[ \phi(v_1^1) \;\le\; j_1 + \tfrac{1}{3}. \]
  • For the target $v_2^0$: by def def-cdmg, item iii., the second endpoint of every directed edge in $E$ lies in $V$, so $v_2 \in V$, and the dichotomy $v_2 \in W$ vs.\ $v_2 \in V \sm W$ is exhaustive. If $v_2 \in W$, then $v_2^0 \in W^0$ is the tagged copy and $\phi(v_2^0) = j_2 - \tfrac{1}{3}$ by the second bullet; if $v_2 \in V \sm W$, then $v_2^0 = v_2$ (the shorthand for unsplit nodes) and $\phi(v_2^0) = \phi(v_2) = j_2$ by the first bullet. In either subcase, \[ \phi(v_2^0) \;\ge\; j_2 - \tfrac{1}{3}. \]

Combining the two displayed bounds with $j_2 - j_1 \ge 1$, \[ \phi(v_1^1) \;\le\; j_1 + \tfrac{1}{3} \;=\; j_2 - \bigl( (j_2 - j_1) - \tfrac{1}{3} \bigr) \;\le\; j_2 - \bigl( 1 - \tfrac{1}{3} \bigr) \;=\; j_2 - \tfrac{2}{3} \;<\; j_2 - \tfrac{1}{3} \;\le\; \phi(v_2^0), \] where the strict inequality $j_2 - \tfrac{2}{3} < j_2 - \tfrac{1}{3}$ is $-\tfrac{2}{3} < -\tfrac{1}{3}$ in $\Q$. Hence $\phi(x) = \phi(v_1^1) < \phi(v_2^0) = \phi(y)$.

In both cases T and L, $\phi(x) < \phi(y)$, completing the proof of Lemma (1).

Proof of sub-claim (b): $<_{\spl}$ is a topological order of $G_{\spl(W)}$.

By def def-topological-order, we must verify that $<_{\spl}$ is a strict total order on $J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$ – i.e.\ irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous on that carrier – and that the parent-precedence clause \[ \forall\, x, y \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)} :\quad x \in \Pa^{G_{\spl(W)}}(y) \;\Longrightarrow\; x <_{\spl} y \] holds.

Strict total order on $J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$. By definition, $x <_{\spl} y \Leftrightarrow \phi(x) < \phi(y)$, with the right-hand $<$ the standard strict order on $\Q$. The standard strict order on $\Q$ is irreflexive, transitive, and trichotomous on $\Q$; we transfer each property to $<_{\spl}$ on $J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$ via the map $\phi : J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)} \to \Q$.

  • Irreflexivity. For every $x \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$, $\phi(x) < \phi(x)$ is false in $\Q$ (irreflexivity of $<$ on $\Q$). Hence $x <_{\spl} x$ is false.
  • Transitivity. For $x, y, z \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$ with $x <_{\spl} y$ and $y <_{\spl} z$, we have $\phi(x) < \phi(y)$ and $\phi(y) < \phi(z)$ in $\Q$, hence $\phi(x) < \phi(z)$ by transitivity of $<$ on $\Q$, i.e.\ $x <_{\spl} z$.
  • Trichotomy. For $x, y \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$, trichotomy of $<$ on $\Q$ applied to $\phi(x), \phi(y)$ gives one of the three alternatives \[ \phi(x) < \phi(y), \quad \phi(x) = \phi(y), \quad \phi(y) < \phi(x). \] The middle alternative $\phi(x) = \phi(y)$ implies $x = y$ by Lemma (0). The other two alternatives say $x <_{\spl} y$ and $y <_{\spl} x$ respectively. Hence one of $x <_{\spl} y$, $x = y$, $y <_{\spl} x$ holds for every pair $x, y \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$.

This verifies that $<_{\spl}$ is a strict total order on $J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$.

Parent-precedence. Let $x, y \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$ with $x \in \Pa^{G_{\spl(W)}}(y)$. By the set-builder definition of the parent set (def def_3_5, item i.) applied to the CDMG $G_{\spl(W)}$, \[ \Pa^{G_{\spl(W)}}(y) \;=\; \lC u \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)} \,\big|\, (u, y) \in E_{\spl(W)} \rC, \] so $(x, y) \in E_{\spl(W)}$. By Lemma (1), $\phi(x) < \phi(y)$, i.e.\ $x <_{\spl} y$.

Both conjuncts of def def-topological-order are verified for $<_{\spl}$ on the CDMG $G_{\spl(W)}$, so $<_{\spl}$ is a topological order of $G_{\spl(W)}$, establishing sub-claim (b).

Proof of sub-claim (a): $G_{\spl(W)}$ is acyclic.

Sub-claim (a) makes no reference to any choice of topological order, so the index map $\phi$ used in Lemmas (0)–(1) above is not provided by the hypotheses of (a) alone. We obtain one as follows. Since $G$ is a CADMG, $G$ is acyclic in the sense of def def-acylic; by the $\Rightarrow$ direction of claim_3_2 applied to $G$, there exists at least one topological order of $G$ in the sense of def def-topological-order. Fix any such order $<$ and present it in its equivalent indexing form (def def-topological-order, “Equivalent indexing form”) as a bijection $\{1, 2, \dots, n\} \to J \cup V$, $i \mapsto v_i$, with $v_1 < v_2 < \cdots < v_n$. With this $<$ and this enumeration in hand, build the index map $\phi : J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)} \to \Q$ exactly as in the statement of sub-claim (b), and apply Lemma (1) (whose statement and proof only require that $<$ is some topological order of $G$): for every $(x, y) \in E_{\spl(W)}$, \[ \phi(x) \;<\; \phi(y). \]

We now argue by contradiction. Suppose, for contradiction, that $G_{\spl(W)}$ admits a non-trivial directed walk from some node to itself, i.e.\ (in the sense of def def:walks, item ii., and def def-acylic) a tuple \[ p \;=\; (x_0, a_0, x_1, a_1, \dots, a_{k-1}, x_k) \] with $k \ge 1$, $x_0 = x_k \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$, each $x_i \in J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)}$, and each $a_i = (x_i, x_{i+1}) \in E_{\spl(W)}$.

By Lemma (1) applied to each edge $a_i = (x_i, x_{i+1}) \in E_{\spl(W)}$, \[ \phi(x_0) \;<\; \phi(x_1) \;<\; \phi(x_2) \;<\; \cdots \;<\; \phi(x_k), \] hence $\phi(x_0) < \phi(x_k)$ by $k \ge 1$ applications of strict transitivity of $<$ on $\Q$ (the chain is non-degenerate since $k \ge 1$ supplies at least one strict step). But $x_0 = x_k$, so $\phi(x_0) = \phi(x_k)$, contradicting the strict inequality $\phi(x_0) < \phi(x_k)$ via irreflexivity of $<$ on $\Q$.

No such walk $p$ can exist, so $G_{\spl(W)}$ is acyclic in the sense of def def-acylic, establishing sub-claim (a).

Remark on an alternative derivation of (a) as a corollary of (b). Once sub-claim (b) is established, sub-claim (a) admits a one-line derivation: pick any topological order $<$ of $G$ (which exists by the $\Rightarrow$ direction of claim_3_2, since $G$ is acyclic by the CADMG hypothesis); by (b), $<_{\spl}$ is a topological order of $G_{\spl(W)}$; and then the $\Leftarrow$ direction of claim_3_2 applied to the CDMG $G_{\spl(W)}$ (which is a CDMG by the construction and consistency check of def def:G_node-splitting) yields acyclicity of $G_{\spl(W)}$. Our direct proof of (a) above is the unwinding of this corollary at the level of the index map $\phi$: it makes the chain $\phi(x_0) < \cdots < \phi(x_k)$ explicit instead of bundling it inside the proof of claim_3_2. The two arguments are equivalent; we have presented the direct version because it makes the index-map $\phi$ the single shared mechanism behind both sub-claims.

Closing remark on the role of the CADMG hypothesis. The CADMG hypothesis on $G$ – i.e.\ acyclicity of $G$ in the sense of def def-acylic – enters the proof of (a) as the precondition for invoking the $\Rightarrow$ direction of claim_3_2 to obtain a topological order $<$ of $G$; sub-claim (b), in contrast, only uses that the supplied $<$ is a topological order, not the acyclicity hypothesis on $G$ directly. The structural hazard flagged in the closing paragraph of the statement block above – a self-loop $(w, w) \in E$ on some $w \in W$ producing the $2$-cycle $w^0 \to w^1 \to w^0$ in $G_{\spl(W)}$ – is ruled out by the same hypothesis: the “no directed self-loops on output nodes” consequence of def def-acylic gives $(w, w) \notin E$ for every $w \in W \ins V$, so the lifted edge $(w^1, w^0)$ never enters $E_{\spl(W)}$. Consistently with this, were $(w, w) \in E$ to hold for some $w \in W$, Case L of Lemma (1) applied to the lifted edge $(w^1, w^0)$ would demand $j_1 < j_2$ for $j_1 = j_2 = j$ (the index of $w = v_j$), which is false; the CADMG hypothesis is precisely what prevents this incoherence from arising upstream.

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on `splOrder`, `splAcyclic`, and `splTopologicalOrder`. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing for this row in particular: the signatures transit through `Finset`-backed graph operations — `G.nodeSplittingOn W hW` (from `def_3_11`), the `Finset` subset `W ⊆ G.V`, and the split graph's parent set — all of which need decidable equality on the element type to compute the resulting `Finset` (and, downstream, decidable equality on `SplitNode Node`, which `def_3_11`'s `deriving DecidableEq` provides automatically). Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g.\ `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not required here and is deferred to downstream consumers that actually use it. Same convention as the rest of `Chapter3_GraphTheory`'s files.

-- Auto-binds `Node` + `DecidableEq Node` as implicits; decidability drives `Finset`-backed splitting operations and the derived `DecidableEq (SplitNode Node)`.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

def splOrder (lt : Node → Node → Prop) :
    SplitNode Node → SplitNode Node → Prop
  | .unsplit u, .unsplit v => lt u v
  | .unsplit u, .copy0 w   => lt u w
  | .unsplit u, .copy1 w   => lt u w ∨ u = w
  | .copy0 v,   .unsplit u => lt v u ∨ v = u
  | .copy0 v,   .copy0 w   => lt v w
  | .copy0 v,   .copy1 w   => lt v w ∨ v = w
  | .copy1 v,   .unsplit u => lt v u
  | .copy1 v,   .copy0 w   => lt v w
  | .copy1 v,   .copy1 w   => lt v w
lean_blocks.explanation: This helper renders the LN's $<_{\spl}$ as a Lean relation on `SplitNode Node`, the three-constructor inductive `def_3_11` uses to encode the disjoint-union carrier $J \cup (V \setminus W) \sqcup W^0 \sqcup W^1$. The input `lt : Node → Node → Prop` is the LN's ambient topological order $<$ on $J \cup V$, kept as a bare binary relation rather than a `[LT Node]` instance so the LN's universal quantification over arbitrary topological orders survives the translation. The body is a nine-case pattern match on the two constructor arguments — one row per pair in `{unsplit, copy0, copy1}` $\times$ `{unsplit, copy0, copy1}` — and is the literal transcription of the LN's $\phi$-based index map after the $\mathbb{Q}$-arithmetic is unwound case by case. Semantically it is the lex order on the pair (base node, copy tag) under the tag ordering `copy0 < unsplit < copy1`: same-tag cases compare via `lt`, mixed-tag cases involving `unsplit` and a `copy0`/`copy1` pull in the equality clause that the $\pm \tfrac{1}{3}$ offset makes the LN's index map yield. As a helper block, `splOrder` exists to give `splTopologicalOrder`'s conclusion `(G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).IsTopologicalOrder (splOrder lt)` a clean named handle, and the same definition is what `splAcyclic`'s proof witnesses internally.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Lean rendering of the LN's `<_{spl}`: a nine-case lex order on `SplitNode Node`, parameterised by the ambient `lt`.
def splOrder (lt : Node → Node → Prop) :
    SplitNode Node → SplitNode Node → Prop
  -- Both `unsplit`: compare base nodes directly via the underlying `lt`.
  | .unsplit u, .unsplit v => lt u v
  -- `unsplit u` vs `copy0 w`: `u`'s integer index `< w - 1/3` reduces to `lt u w`.
  | .unsplit u, .copy0 w   => lt u w
  -- `unsplit u` vs `copy1 w`: `u < w + 1/3` allows the boundary equality `u = w`.
  | .unsplit u, .copy1 w   => lt u w ∨ u = w
  -- `copy0 v` vs `unsplit u`: symmetric to the case above, equality at the boundary.
  | .copy0 v,   .unsplit u => lt v u ∨ v = u
  -- Both `copy0`: the `- 1/3` offsets cancel; reduces to `lt v w`.
  | .copy0 v,   .copy0 w   => lt v w
  -- `copy0 v` vs `copy1 w`: same base node `v = w` gives `copy0 v < copy1 v` (the transfer edge orientation).
  | .copy0 v,   .copy1 w   => lt v w ∨ v = w
  -- `copy1 v` vs `unsplit u`: `v + 1/3 < u` forces strict `lt v u`.
  | .copy1 v,   .unsplit u => lt v u
  -- `copy1 v` vs `copy0 w`: `+1/3` vs `-1/3` offset still leaves a strict `lt v w` requirement.
  | .copy1 v,   .copy0 w   => lt v w
  -- Both `copy1`: the `+ 1/3` offsets cancel; reduces to `lt v w`.
  | .copy1 v,   .copy1 w   => lt v w

theorem splAcyclic (G : CDMG Node) (hCADMG : G.IsCADMG)
    (W : Finset Node) (hW : W ⊆ G.V) :
    (G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).IsAcyclic
lean_blocks.explanation: This theorem formalises sub-claim (a) of the LN remark: the node-split graph $G_{\spl(W)}$ inherits acyclicity from the CADMG hypothesis on $G$. The signature names match the LN almost verbatim — `G : CDMG Node` is the LN's $G = (J, V, E, L)$, `hCADMG : G.IsCADMG` is the LN-named CADMG attribute from `def_3_7` (which unfolds to `IsAcyclic` plus a bidirected-edge constraint), `W : Finset Node` with `hW : W ⊆ G.V` is the LN's $W \subseteq V$, and `G.nodeSplittingOn W hW` is `def_3_11`'s construction of $G_{\spl(W)}$. The conclusion `(G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).IsAcyclic` is `def_3_6`'s acyclicity predicate dot-applied to the split CDMG. The CADMG hypothesis is load-bearing: without it, a directed self-loop $(w, w) \in E$ for some $w \in W$ would, under `def_3_11`'s edge conventions, manufacture a $2$-cycle $w^0 \to w^1 \to w^0$ in the split graph. This block is paired with `splTopologicalOrder` below — sub-claim (b) — and the proof of `splAcyclic` actually routes through the topological-order construction via the equivalence `acyclic_iff_topological_order` (`claim_3_4`), feeding `splOrder lt` into the right-to-left direction. Each sub-claim is kept as its own theorem so downstream SWIG-style consumers can invoke acyclicity preservation without dragging in a topological order they do not need.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Sub-claim (a): node-splitting preserves acyclicity, packaged so downstream consumers can invoke it without `lt`.
theorem splAcyclic (G : CDMG Node) (hCADMG : G.IsCADMG)
    -- LN's `W ⊆ V` precondition; `Finset.subset` here, with `hW` supplied to `nodeSplittingOn`.
    (W : Finset Node) (hW : W ⊆ G.V) :
    -- Conclusion: `def_3_6`'s `IsAcyclic` predicate dot-applied to `def_3_11`'s split CDMG.
    (G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).IsAcyclic

lean_blocks.code:
theorem splTopologicalOrder (G : CDMG Node) (hCADMG : G.IsCADMG)
    (W : Finset Node) (hW : W ⊆ G.V)
    (lt : Node → Node → Prop) (hlt : G.IsTopologicalOrder lt) :
    (G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).IsTopologicalOrder (splOrder lt)
lean_blocks.explanation: This theorem formalises sub-claim (b): given any topological order `lt` on the original CADMG, the lifted relation `splOrder lt` (the helper above) is itself a topological order of the node-split graph $G_{\spl(W)}$. The signature extends `splAcyclic`'s prefix `(G, hCADMG, W, hW)` with the two extra inputs sub-claim (b) needs — `lt : Node → Node → Prop` and `hlt : G.IsTopologicalOrder lt` (per `def_3_8`) — and concludes `(G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).IsTopologicalOrder (splOrder lt)`, reusing `def_3_8`'s predicate verbatim against the split CDMG (it is polymorphic in the node type, so no `IsTopologicalOrderOnSplit` variant is needed). The `IsTopologicalOrder` content packages strict-total-order axioms with parent-precedence; the latter is the load-bearing part, since every edge of $E_{\spl(W)}$ is either a lifted edge $(v_1^1, v_2^0)$ from $(v_1, v_2) \in G.E$ or a transfer edge $(w^0, w^1)$ for $w \in W$, and `splOrder` is shaped to satisfy parent-precedence on both. This block is the companion of `splAcyclic` above: the two sub-claims of the LN's $\mathrm{Rem}$ are stated as separate theorems, and `splAcyclic`'s proof in fact consumes a witness of this form internally to discharge acyclicity via `acyclic_iff_topological_order`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Sub-claim (b): the lifted `splOrder lt` is a topological order of `G_{spl(W)}`; companion to `splAcyclic`.
theorem splTopologicalOrder (G : CDMG Node) (hCADMG : G.IsCADMG)
    -- Same `(W, hW)` block as `splAcyclic`, feeding `nodeSplittingOn`.
    (W : Finset Node) (hW : W ⊆ G.V)
    -- LN's `<` on `J ∪ V`, bare `Node → Node → Prop`; `hlt` carries `def_3_8`'s axioms.
    (lt : Node → Node → Prop) (hlt : G.IsTopologicalOrder lt) :
    -- Reuses `def_3_8`'s predicate against the split CDMG, applied to the helper `splOrder lt`.
    (G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).IsTopologicalOrder (splOrder lt)

design_choices:
**Case-analysis encoding of `<_{\spl}`, not $\mathbb{Q}$-arithmetic.** The LN defines $<_{\spl}$ via an index map $\phi : J_{\spl(W)} \cup V_{\spl(W)} \to \mathbb{Q}$ assigning $j \pm \tfrac{1}{3}$ to the tagged copies and $j$ to unsplit nodes. We encode `splOrder` directly as a nine-case pattern match on the two `SplitNode` constructors. Three reasons. (i) The $\mathbb{Q}$ formulation reuses no Mathlib infrastructure: every proof step would need to thread through a custom $\mathrm{idx} : \mathrm{Node} \to \mathbb{N}$ axiom and $\mathbb{Q}$-coercions. (ii) The lex structure on (base node, copy tag) is structurally visible in the case analysis but hidden under the $\mathbb{Q}$-arithmetic surface. (iii) The case-analysis form sidesteps the subtlety that the LN's text leaves indices of unsplit nodes implicit — the first constructor pair makes "unsplit nodes compare via the underlying $<$" literal.

**`W` does not appear in `splOrder`'s signature.** A naive translation would parameterise `splOrder` by $W$, but the lex form depends only on `lt` and the constructor tags. The $W$-dependence is absorbed into the carrier `SplitNode Node` (each constructor is formally distinct regardless of $W$-membership), and the $W$-dependent membership facts live in the underlying CDMG `G.nodeSplittingOn W hW`. Threading $W$ through `splOrder` would force every call site to project it back out.

**Lex orientation $\mathrm{copy0} < \mathrm{unsplit} < \mathrm{copy1}$ is load-bearing.** It is forced by `def_3_11`'s edge conventions: the transfer edge added is $(w^0, w^1)$, every incoming edge onto a split node is reattached at the $\mathrm{copy0}$ tag, and every outgoing edge leaves the $\mathrm{copy1}$ tag. Parent-precedence in any topological order of the split graph therefore demands this orientation at each base node. Under the opposite tagging convention, the LN's symmetric $j \mp \tfrac{1}{3}$ index assignment would not produce a topological order — sub-claim (b) is correct as stated precisely because the index choice matches the tagging convention.

**Two separate theorems, not one bundled biconjunction.** The LN bundles (a) and (b) inside one $\mathrm{Rem}$, but the sub-claims have different downstream consumers: hard-intervention-then-node-split SWIG constructions need only acyclicity preservation. A bundled $\mathrm{splAcyclic} \land \mathrm{splTopologicalOrder}$-shaped theorem would force every consumer to take an unused topological-order argument or pattern-match through `.1` at every call site. Splitting also matches the chapter convention of one atomic predicate per sub-statement.

**`G.IsCADMG`, not `G.IsAcyclic`, as hypothesis.** The LN reads "For a CADMG $G$". Although `IsCADMG` unfolds to `IsAcyclic` plus a bidirected-edge constraint, taking the LN-named attribute keeps the Lean signature greppable from the LN without a translation step.

**`lt` as a bare `Node → Node → Prop`.** The LN universally quantifies over an arbitrary topological order. A `[LinearOrder Node]` / `[LT Node]` typeclass formulation would lock in a single canonical order per `Node` type, colliding with the LN's parameterisation by *any* topological order, and would force every consumer to manufacture an instance even when they only have a one-off witness from `claim_3_4`.
